core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_3 because its opening Stone-first frame became the durable consensus baseline, while Agent_3's preferred Jewel+Apple upgrade stayed conditional on explicit consent. The process advantage was real but limited: Agent_2 captured the highest final utility and Agent_3's veto forced Jewel+Quill into the final deal.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1 spoke first, repeatedly anchored Stone as the must-have item, and proposed Option C early; by Round 3 every proposal copied that allocation. Counterexample: the advantage was not unilateral, since Agent_3 could reject no-Jewel proposals and Agent_2 finished with the highest utility.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by declaring Stone its top priority and offering compensation around that anchor. Agent_2 and Agent_3 stated their own priorities, but Agent_1's Option C became the repeated public baseline: Stone to Agent_1, Apple+Pencil to Agent_2, Jewel+Quill to Agent_3. Agent_3 preferred Jewel+Apple but accepted the faster baseline.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Raw private thinking from all agents converged on Option C as the most robust path, with Variant X treated as a fallback only if all three explicitly consented. This shows the public anchor became an internal coordination point, not just surface rhetoric.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 had Agent_1 and Agent_3 proposing Option C while Agent_2 proposed a self-favoring allocation. Round 2 proposals over-concentrated items with Agent_1/Agent_2 and failed. In Round 3, all three formal proposals were identical Option C allocations.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private votes show strategic utility maximization and vetoes: Agent_2 rejected lower-utility Option C in Round 1, and Agent_3 rejected Round 2 allocations that gave it nothing. In Round 3, all agents accepted the identical baseline; Agent_1 secured 50.22 and Agent_3 accepted 40.5.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections consistently identified Stone-to-Agent_1 and Pencil+Apple-to-Agent_2 as stable, with Jewel/Apple as the main sticking point. They recommended timeboxed Option C publication and kept Variant X conditional on explicit consent, reinforcing the baseline that favored Agent_1 over Agent_3.

mechanisms.theme: First-speaker Stone anchor became the agenda
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public turn to make Stone the central condition of any deal, then quickly translated that anchor into Option C. Later discussion treated Stone-to-Agent_1 as stable.

evidence.quote: My top priority: Stone (value 62 to me).
as long as Stone ends up with me
Proposed public path (Option C): 3-way with me as Stone recipient

mechanisms.theme: Option C became the focal proposal copied by all
mechanisms.explanation: The final agreement did not merely give Agent_1 Stone; it replicated the allocation Agent_1 had put forward early. By Round 3, all proposals were identical and consensus followed.

evidence.quote: I propose: {'Agent_1': [2], 'Agent_2': [0, 4], 'Agent_3': [1, 3]}
I propose: {'Agent_1': [2], 'Agent_2': [0, 4], 'Agent_3': [1, 3]}
I propose: {'Agent_1': [2], 'Agent_2': [0, 4], 'Agent_3': [1, 3]}
CONSENSUS REACHED! Proposal #3 accepted unanimously!

mechanisms.theme: Last speaker's preferred upgrade stayed conditional
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3 stated that Jewel+Apple would be better than Jewel+Quill, but the negotiation made that upgrade dependent on Agent_2 and full group consent. Agent_3 therefore accepted the faster baseline rather than extracting the higher-value variant.

evidence.quote: Jewel + Quill (50 total) versus Jewel + Apple (54 total)
I’d personally prefer Jewel + Apple to maximize my own payoff
Without that consent, I’ll stick with Option C to avoid stalling.
Variant X stays as a tightly controlled fallback

mechanisms.theme: Private reasoning reinforced the public anchor
mechanisms.explanation: The raw private-thinking entries from multiple agents internalized the same stable baseline, with Stone preserved for Agent_1 and the Jewel/Apple reweighting relegated to a consent-only fallback.

evidence.quote: the most stable path is to allocate Stone to Agent_1
The most robust path to unanimous acceptance appears to be a fast baseline
the most robust baseline is Option C

mechanisms.explanation: The first position did not simply steamroll the last position. When Round 2 proposals left Agent_3 with nothing, Agent_3 rejected them and later reflections recognized that Jewel had to be allocated to Agent_3 for consensus.

evidence.quote: Agent_3 receives no items in this allocation.
No proposal achieved unanimous acceptance.
my top priority Jewel isn't allocated to me
which is why they rejected every option

mechanisms.theme: Utility gap also reflects item values, not only order
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's numerical edge over Agent_3 came from Stone's high value to Agent_1 and discounting, while Agent_2 actually finished ahead of both. This weakens any claim that first position alone explains the outcome.

evidence.quote: This preserves top priorities and enables quick closure.
